message:
perf(mvcc,core,vdbe): Hekaton-style lock-free page locks, cached read snapshots, covering-index fast path, lifecycle optimizations

Four performance improvements targeting the MVCC hot path, read-heavy
workloads, and index-driven query execution.

Hekaton-style lock-free page locks (core_types.rs):

- Pages 1..=65536 now use a flat AtomicU64 array (512 KiB) for
  lock-free CAS-based try_acquire/release. Zero Mutex contention
  on the hot path — a single compare_exchange replaces the previous
  shard lookup + Mutex lock + HashMap get/insert.

- Re-entrant acquisition detected by comparing CAS failure value
  against the requesting TxnId. Contention still emits observability
  events via emit_page_lock_contention.

- Pages > 65536 fall back to the existing sharded Mutex+HashMap.

- Rolling rebuild draining check guarded by `has_draining` AtomicBool
  (Relaxed load) so the draining Mutex is never touched during normal
  operation — rebuilds are maintenance-only.

Cached read-only pager snapshots (connection.rs):

- When an autocommit read-only SELECT completes, the pager transaction
  is parked in `cached_read_snapshot` instead of being committed.
  The next autocommit read reuses this snapshot — skipping
  pager.begin() + pager.commit() entirely.

- Invalidated on any write, DDL, explicit BEGIN, or schema cookie
  change. Hot-path profile counters track reuses and parks.

- This eliminates per-statement pager transaction overhead for
  consecutive read-only queries (common in analytics and read replicas).

Transaction lifecycle optimizations (lifecycle.rs):

- Snapshot read simplified: removed verbose tracing span wrapper
  around version store resolve, using direct resolve() call. The
  tracing overhead was measurable on high-frequency read paths.

- Write-set membership check in write_page_in_place changed from
  O(n) Vec::contains to O(1) HashMap::contains_key on write_set_data.

- Write-set membership in write_page_concurrent uses the `newly_locked`
  flag from try_acquire result instead of a separate Vec::contains
  scan — if the page was newly locked, it cannot already be in the
  write set.

Covering-index fast path (codegen.rs):

- Index equality scan now skips OpenRead on the table cursor when
  all output columns can be satisfied from the index alone (covering
  index). Separate fast_path_done_label ensures only the index cursor
  is closed on the fast path.

- Full table cursor is opened only in the full-scan fallback path,
  avoiding unnecessary table B-tree traversal for covered queries.

Conformance:

- Un-ignored test_conformance_trigger_before_update_s74y — the prior
  trigger column resolution fix resolved the underlying issue.

Also updates beads issue tracker metadata.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"bd-db300.10","title":"Track J: revised baseline engine tax reduction with arenas, caches, and data-motion cuts","description":"## Goal
Close the broad per-operation efficiency gap versus SQLite even when contention is not the dominant problem.

## Background
The matrix proves that MVCC-only thinking is insufficient. FrankenSQLite is also too slow at `c1`, on mixed rows with low retry pressure, and in forced single-writer mode. That means the engine still burns too much work in parser/planner reuse, decode/materialization, page staging, ownership transitions, and transient allocation/copy churn.

## Scope
This track covers:
- baseline truthing for cache-hit and hot-path reuse rates
- arenas/lookaside storage and reusable page buffers
- prepared-statement, plan, and decode caches
- PageData ownership and data-motion reduction
- VDBE fast paths, reusable frames, and safe read-path improvements
- build/profile hygiene for honest perf binaries
- explicit verification across c1, mixed, and single-writer rows with structured logs

## Guardrails
- baseline work must improve both MVCC and single-writer when it touches shared code
- avoid giant mixed-scope patches; each child bead should target one measured waste family
- keep correctness and invalidation semantics explicit whenever reuse is introduced

## Acceptance Criteria
Success means:
- the track covers memory reuse, plan reuse, decode reuse, PageData/data-motion cleanup, VDBE fast paths, and perf-binary hygiene
- the track explicitly carries verification obligations for unit tests, e2e scripts, structured logging, and benchmark attribution
- the track is self-contained enough that future agents can use it without reopening the original revised-plan markdown","acceptance_criteria":"Success means:
- the track covers memory reuse, plan reuse, decode reuse, PageData/data-motion cleanup, VDBE fast paths, and perf-binary hygiene
- the track explicitly carries verification obligations for unit tests, e2e scripts, structured logging, and benchmark attribution
- the track is self-contained enough that future agents can use it without reopening the original revised-plan markdown","notes":"Planning note:
This track corresponds to Phase 5 of the revised plan, but starts after the minimal truthing pass rather than waiting for all structural work to finish. It exists because the matrix shows a separate baseline-efficiency deficit that would still matter even if the concurrent-writer story were perfect.

Cross-track rule:
- Track J is parallel to Track I by design; it is not allowed to hide behind structural work, and structural work is not allowed to use baseline-tax progress as an excuse to leave convoy pathologies unresolved

User-facing success condition:
- a future operator or agent should be able to explain, from this track alone, which non-structural taxes were removed, which rows improved as a result, and which verification bundle proves the win without reopening old sessions","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T23:07:33.425124255Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-03-15T00:52:17.089470179Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["allocator","baseline-tax","db300-program","db300-track-j","memory-management","perf","sqlite","track-j","vdbe"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-db300.10","depends_on_id":"bd-db300","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T23:07:33.425124255Z","created_by":"codex","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2284,"issue_id":"bd-db300.10","author":"ubuntu","text":"Clause coverage:
- Covers ES.2, ES.6, Phase 5 umbrella intent, O.6, and the cross-track rule that baseline-tax work must proceed in parallel rather than hiding behind structural work.
- This is the authoritative baseline-tax umbrella for Phase 5 and its proof burden.","created_at":"2026-03-15T00:43:33Z"}]}

The catastrophic `disjoint c8` row is not primarily parser/decode/materialization; the hot-profile pack only attributes ~6-7 ms to those subsystems inside a ~10.4 s run. But the matrix still shows `c1`, mixed, and forced single-writer rows are broadly too slow, so baseline tax remains a separate problem even if E2/E5 fix the concurrency-collapse regime.

Practical implication: keep Track J focused on shared-code costs that help both MVCC and single-writer: `PageData` clone/copy reduction in `SharedTxnPageIo`, reusable page-image buffers, prepared-artifact reuse, and the pager-side de-serialization path (`bd-1dp9.6.7.7`). Alien-fit candidates with reasonable adoption wedges are LeanStore-style direct page-handle/pointer-swizzle fast paths and more aggressive page-buffer reuse, not broad JIT/vectorization work first. Fallback trigger: if Stage 4/5 and structural fixes unexpectedly eliminate most low-concurrency loss too, re-rank Track J behind the remaining verified limiter instead of assuming baseline tax is still first-order.","created_at":"2026-03-18T13:43:44Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-db300.10.1","title":"J1: instrument baseline cache-hit, reuse, and hot-path waste rates for the non-structural engine gap","description":"## Goal
Produce the baseline-tax truthing layer that complements the structural truthing pass.

## Scope
- measure statement-plan cache hit rate, prepared-artifact reuse rate, decode-cache hit rate, cursor/frame reuse rate, page-buffer reuse, and other non-structural reuse signals
- attribute non-structural waste across parser/planner churn, VDBE setup, record decode, row materialization, PageData ownership churn, and transient allocation/copy cost
- focus on c1 rows, mixed rows with low retry pressure, and forced single-writer rows where baseline cost is easiest to isolate

## Acceptance Criteria
Success means:
- representative low-retry rows expose stable cache-hit and reuse metrics plus a baseline waste ledger
- the ledger makes clear which costs are structural-convoy side effects and which are true baseline-tax problems
- unit tests cover metric aggregation and edge cases such as cold-cache startup versus steady state
- the deliverable includes a ranked summary of the dominant baseline waste buckets for c1, mixed low-retry, and forced single-writer rows","acceptance_criteria":"Success means:
- representative low-retry rows expose stable cache-hit and reuse metrics plus a baseline waste ledger
- the ledger makes clear which costs are structural-convoy side effects and which are true baseline-tax problems
- unit tests cover metric aggregation and edge cases such as cold-cache startup versus steady state
- the deliverable includes a ranked summary of the dominant baseline waste buckets for c1, mixed low-retry, and forced single-writer rows","notes":"Coordination note:
J1 should begin as soon as the A3 evidence leaves are ready; it must not wait for structural-truthing conclusions because the revised plan explicitly treats baseline tax as a parallel first-wave concern.

Verification/logging requirement:
- require a benchmark-output smoke check on c1, one mixed low-retry row, and one forced single-writer row
- record stable structured log families for baseline_waste_ledger, cache_hit_ledger, and allocation_copy_summary so later baseline beads can cite them directly

 {"id":"bd-db300.10.10","title":"J10: verify baseline-tax reductions across c1, mixed, and single-writer rows with attribution-quality evidence","description":"## Goal
Force the baseline track to prove real wins rather than hiding behind architectural intent.

## Scope
- map each baseline implementation bead to concrete unit tests, e2e scripts, and structured log fields
- require before/after evidence on c1, mixed low-retry, and forced single-writer rows where baseline cost is easiest to isolate
- attribute observed wins, neutral results, and regressions to the specific baseline lever that caused them
- keep the result ready for the canonical matrix scorecard and the project-level narrative about where the remaining gap still comes from
- require exact rerun entrypoints and operator-facing triage steps for each baseline verification bundle

## Acceptance Criteria
Success means:
- every baseline implementation bead has named unit tests, named e2e scripts, expected artifact paths, and required structured log event names recorded
- the verification plan includes invalidation, reuse-boundary, reset/rollback, and stale-data negative cases where relevant
- attribution-quality benchmark artifacts exist for the major baseline levers
- the verification bundle makes clear which baseline ideas moved c1, mixed, or single-writer rows and which did not","acceptance_criteria":"Success means:
- every baseline implementation bead has named unit tests, named e2e scripts, expected artifact paths, and required structured log event names recorded
- the verification plan includes invalidation, reuse-boundary, reset/rollback, and stale-data negative cases where relevant
- attribution-quality benchmark artifacts exist for the major baseline levers
- the verification bundle makes clear which baseline ideas moved c1, mixed, or single-writer rows and which did not","notes":"Verification bundle requirements:
- for each baseline implementation bead, record the exact unit/property test modules or scenario families, the required e2e harness/script entrypoints, the expected structured log event families, and the artifact bundle keys or directories
- require before/after focused reruns for arenas/lookaside, page-buffer reuse, plan cache, decode cache, PageData churn, copy-heavy kernel reductions, VDBE fast paths, and perf-binary hygiene
- preserve negative and neutral outcomes with the same schema so the team can distinguish real wins from noise or optimism

Closure standard:
- a future agent should be able to rerun the baseline proof set from this bead and identify which levers moved c1, mixed, and forced single-writer rows without reopening planning  {"id":"bd-db300.10.2","title":"J2: add statement and transaction-local arenas or lookaside storage for parser, VDBE, and materialization scratch","description":"## Goal
Reduce general-allocator churn on the hot execution path.

## Scope
- adopt explicit statement/transaction-local arenas or lookaside storage for parser objects, VDBE frame scratch, row materialization scratch, semantic/structural intent scratch where applicable, and other small transient buffers
- make ownership and reset boundaries explicit at reset/commit/rollback
- prefer wholesale arena drop/reset at lifecycle boundaries over repeated general-allocator churn for small transient objects
- add unit tests for reset safety, reuse safety, and no-leak/no-stale-data semantics
- add benchmark evidence showing lower allocation pressure and improved c1/mixed rows

## Acceptance Criteria

- the targeted scratch/work objects move off the general allocator hot path
- reset/rollback semantics are explicit and covered by tests
- focused benchmarks show materially reduced allocation churn and improved baseline rows","acceptance_criteria":"Success means:
